id: m_summarizedMemory_v4_NOISE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
scriptId: cs_v4_NOISE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use
historyId: h_v4_NOISE_L40_Excessive phone or social media use

memories.content: A practical, short plan to start tomorrow was provided, with these steps: Tonight (before sleep) put the phone in another room or on Do Not Disturb and enable app limits for the top 1–2 social apps (30–60 min/day); Morning (first 30 minutes) avoid the phone and use that time to stretch, plan the day, or have coffee; Work blocks use Pomodoro (25/5), keep the phone out of reach during work intervals, and turn on Focus/Do Not Disturb

The conversation block includes a Message requesting avoiding named apps, subscriptions, or brand-specific tools. The Response acknowledges the request and provides five actions to reduce phone use: 1) Tonight: charge phone in another room and silence notifications until morning. 2) Morning: no phone for the first 30 minutes—stretch, plan, or have coffee. 3) Work: 25 minutes focused work with 5-minute breaks; keep phone out of reach. 4) Allow two 15-minute social-check windows with social notifications silenced otherwise. 5) One hour before bed: screens off; read or listen; keep phone outside the bedroom.
User states they are in their late 20s and work full-time from home; assistant responds with thanks and says five low-cost behavioral steps will fit with working from home and can be tried tomorrow.
A plan was provided to address the user’s worst times (commute, lunch breaks, and the hour before bed) consisting of five actions: 1) Commute — put phone in bag

The user stated that boredom and stress are usual triggers for scrolling, sometimes not realizing they’re scrolling; the response acknowledged this and recommended a 2-minute grounding routine (breath, stretch, walk) and keeping the phone out of reach or in a closed drawer during commute, lunch, and the hour before bed.

User expressed a preference for practical steps over long explanations or academic studies; assistant acknowledged this, said it would keep responses short and practical, and offered five concise, one-line actions targeting the user's commute, lunch, and pre-bed.

User attempted a phone-free weekend and reached Saturday evening before giving in; the response praised the progress, advised a 15-minute Saturday-evening check-in next time to avoid deprivation, and suggested a concrete substitute that evening (walk, movie, cook, or meet a friend).

User reports their cat knocking a phone off the couch and views it as a deterrent; the responder says this is amusing and handy, and suggests either leaning into it as a nudge or keeping the phone in a closed drawer or another room to keep it out of sight.

A user asked whether people check their phones over 100 times daily; the response stated it is possibly true for many but not universal, with some people checking only a few dozen times daily and heavy users exceeding 100; headlines tend to cite the high end; to know for an individual, check the phone’s built-in screen-time/digital-wellbeing report for pickups and app use.

The message states that neighbors leave their phones in another room during dinner, which makes the meal less rushed; the response says this habit is simple and effective and can make dinners calmer and more present.

A coworker swapped social feeds for podcasts and said it reduced mindless scrolling; the responder called it a smart move and suggested trying short podcasts or playlists during commute, lunch, or chores to replace feed-checking.

Message stated that a friend replaced apps with books and admitted audiobooks became autopilot; the response said audio can feel autopilot and recommended alternating with physical books, pausing to jot one takeaway, or setting deliberate stopping points.

A person says they used to set timers but would ignore them and felt limits were easy to bypass; in response, six strategies are suggested to make scrolling harder: put the phone in another room or a closed drawer during focus; remove social apps from the home screen or log out temporarily; use a physical kitchen timer for work/break cycles and keep the phone away; pre-commit to a friend to check in if you slip; perform a 2-minute substitute (walk, stretch, drink water) when the urge hits; turn the screen grayscale and silence banners to reduce visual pull.

A user states that opening an app when stressed feels like a tiny reward and breaking that loop is tough; the assistant replies that the reward loop is normal and provides five tactics: a 2-minute grounding routine (slow breaths and a stretch) before unlocking; add friction by keeping the phone in a closed drawer or face-down; replace the check with a 2-minute substitute (walk, tea, or step outside); pre-commit to one small task before any social check; and use a 10-minute wait rule by setting a kitchen timer to delay the urge.

User suggested that changing wallpaper to plain would reduce pull. The response stated plain wallpapers lower visual stimulation and offered four tips: set a neutral, low-contrast wallpaper (solid color or subtle texture); simplify the lock screen and add a reminder like “Wait 10 minutes”; move social apps off the home screen into a folder to make them harder to reach; pair with silenced notifications or grayscale for a stronger effect.

Discussion about shifting notifications to a smartwatch: it can help by enabling quick glances to triage without unlocking the phone and by keeping the phone out of sight, but it can also move the habit to the wrist and increase micro-interruptions; practical setup recommended includes forwarding only high-priority channels to the watch, muting social feeds and banners, using subtle vibration, requiring opening the phone for full content, and trying it for a few days to see whether phone pickups drop or wrist-checks multiply.
